examples.title: Universal Quantifier Proof Pattern
examples.description: Algorithm demonstrating how to prove a universal statement
examples.explanation: To prove a universal statement, we pick an arbitrary element from the domain, prove the property holds for it, and then generalize. The key is that the element must be arbitrary (not special).

examples.title: Existential Quantifier Proof Pattern
examples.description: Algorithm demonstrating how to prove an existential statement
examples.explanation: To prove an existential statement, we must produce (construct) a specific witness that satisfies the predicate. Simply showing ¬∀x ¬P(x) is insufficient in constructive mathematics.

examples.title: Negation of Quantifiers Implementation
examples.description: Shows how to negate quantified statements programmatically
examples.explanation: This demonstrates De Morgan's laws for quantifiers. When negating, flip the quantifier and negate the predicate. This is essential for proof by contradiction.

examples.title: Quantifier Order Verification
examples.description: Algorithm to check truth of nested quantifier statements
examples.code:
// Checking truth of ∀x∃y P(x,y) vs ∃y∀x P(x,y)
// P(x,y): x + y = 0, Domain: Integers

FUNCTION checkForAllExists(D, P):
 // ∀x∃y P(x,y): For every x, there exists y
 FOR EACH x IN D:
 found ← FALSE
 FOR EACH y IN D:
 IF P(x, y) = TRUE THEN:
 found ← TRUE
 BREAK // Found y for this x
 END IF
 END FOR
 IF found = FALSE THEN:
 RETURN FALSE // Counterexample found
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE

FUNCTION checkExistsForAll(D, P):
 // ∃y∀x P(x,y): There exists y that works for all x
 FOR EACH y IN D:
 allTrue ← TRUE
 FOR EACH x IN D:
 IF P(x, y) = FALSE THEN:
 allTrue ← FALSE
 BREAK // This y doesn't work for all x
 END IF
 END FOR
 IF allTrue = TRUE THEN:
 RETURN TRUE // Found the y
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN FALSE

// For x+y=0 with domain integers:
// checkForAllExists: TRUE (choose y=-x)
// checkExistsForAll: FALSE (no single y works for all x)
examples.explanation: This illustrates the fundamental difference between ∀x∃y and ∃y∀x. The first checks each row for some column; the second checks if there's a column that works for all rows.
